main_summary.summary: Robert McClary, interviewed by Dr. Hasan Kwame Jeffries on March 9, 2013 in Albany, Georgia, recounts his entry into the Albany Movement and his fieldwork with Charles Sherrod and SNCC across Southwest Georgia. Born at Phoebe Putney Hospital, McClary describes a childhood shaped by his father's physical confrontation with a white man in South Carolina and the family's subsequent migration. He recalls early schooling in the segregated South and describes how local desegregation pressure pulled him into organizing. With Sherrod he worked on tenant rights, welfare-denial appeals, and a plan to organize roughly nine Southwest Georgia counties to elect Black officials locally. He recounts being attacked while sleeping in a SNCC office and notes a fellow staff member's intervention before police removed the attacker. He references a school-board confrontation in a small Southwest Georgia town and the eventual election of its first Black mayor. Closing the interview, he says he felt more hopeful and excited than afraid. The transcript is heavily degraded by automatic-speech-recognition failure; many passages survive only as fragments and topical anchors rather than continuous testimony.
key_themes: SNCC field organizing in Southwest Georgia
main_summary.historical_significance:
McClary's testimony documents grassroots SNCC field work in Southwest Georgia under Charles Sherrod's leadership, including the multi-county voter and electoral strategy and the day-to-day tenant and welfare-appeals casework that sustained the project between mass-meeting moments. His firsthand account of an assault inside a SNCC office, and of a staff colleague intervening before police removed the attacker, adds a primary-source data point to the record of physical risk borne by local field staff.

The interview's value as a primary source is constrained by severe automatic-transcription degradation: large portions of the recording survive in the transcript only as repeated phrases, fragmentary cues, or unrecoverable runs. The anchored facts above are corroborated through audit-confirmed corrections against ground-truth references and the Library of Congress canonical archive, but extended narrative reconstruction from this transcript is not advisable without returning to the source audio.

chapters.title: Interview Opening
chapters.summary: Dr. Hasan Kwame Jeffries introduces the March 9, 2013 interview in Albany, Georgia for the Civil Rights History Project, a joint Smithsonian NMAAHC and Library of Congress undertaking. He identifies videographer Petna Ndaliko and welcomes Robert McClary.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project oral history series

chapters.title: Birth and Early Family
chapters.summary: McClary states he was born at Phoebe Putney Hospital on October 19. He references his mother's death and his father taking a job on the river before the family moved. Much of this passage is unrecoverable in the transcript due to severe automatic-speech-recognition degradation.

chapters.title: Father's Confrontation in South Carolina
chapters.summary: McClary recounts that his father had a physical confrontation with a white man in South Carolina before leaving the state. He later reports uncertain news that his father is dead. The transcript preserves only fragments of this account.

chapters.title: Schooling and Early Desegregation
chapters.summary: McClary describes attending school and references early local desegregation attempts involving Black families. He mentions a young woman who had a confrontation with a white man on a bus. The passage is heavily corrupted by transcription failure.

chapters.title: Entry into the Albany Movement
chapters.summary: McClary states that he was already involved in the movement before the period he had been describing. He references the formation of the Albany Movement and his initial contact with Charles Sherrod.

chapters.title: Southwest Georgia County Strategy
chapters.summary: McClary describes Charles Sherrod's effort to organize roughly nine counties in Southwest Georgia, including a plan to take over one of the counties and elect officials locally. He notes that Sherrod was an activist by disposition and describes joining the project formally.

chapters.title: Tenants and Welfare-Rights Work
chapters.summary: After a brief technical break, McClary describes movement activities centered on tenants and welfare casework. He references advocating for residents who were being denied welfare and filing appeals when claims were taken down. Specific details are largely lost to transcription degradation.

chapters.title: Casino Desegregation Attempt
chapters.summary: McClary recounts an attempt with a white activist visiting from New York to desegregate a casino-named cafe in his hometown. He describes the proprietor's reaction and the standoff at the establishment. The exchange is preserved only in fragmentary form.

chapters.title: Assault at the SNCC Office
chapters.summary: McClary describes being attacked while sleeping in a SNCC office. He recounts being struck and ending up on the floor, then convincing his attacker he could not produce a specific person. A fellow staff member intervened, and the police took the attacker away.

chapters.title: Norway School Board and First Black Mayor
chapters.summary: McClary recalls a school-board confrontation in Norway, a small Southwest Georgia town, and the eventual election of the town's first Black mayor, who has since died. He describes mobilizing community members to the local board.
related_events: first Black mayor election in Norway

chapters.title: Closing Reflection on Hope and Fear
chapters.summary: Asked whether he was ever afraid, McClary says he was more hopeful and excited than afraid. He returns to his father's example, noting that the older man would not back down from a physical confrontation. Jeffries closes the interview.

loc_video.caption: Robert McClary oral history interview conducted by Hasan Kwame Jeffries in Albany, Georgia, 2013 March 09
